q: What are the primary use cases for Azure Arc-enabled SQL Server and SQL Managed Instance, and when would you choose Arc over a native Azure SQL deployment?
a: Azure Arc-enabled SQL services extend Azure management plane capabilities to SQL Server instances running outside Azure—on-premises, on AWS EC2, Google Cloud VMs, or edge locations. The key use cases are: unified governance and compliance across hybrid estates (Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Azure Policy, Azure Monitor all apply), enabling Microsoft Entra ID authentication on on-premises SQL Server, getting automated patching and best-practices assessment without migrating, and running SQL Managed Instance enabled by Azure Arc on Kubernetes clusters (on-prem or any cloud) for containerised workloads that must stay outside Azure regions. You choose Arc when data residency, regulatory, or latency constraints prohibit moving to Azure PaaS, but you still want Azure-native tooling. Arc-enabled SQL MI runs in a Kubernetes operator model, supporting Business Critical and General Purpose tiers, and supports contained availability groups for HA inside the cluster.
fj: Azure Arc-enabled SQL is only useful for legacy SQL Server versions that cannot be upgraded to Azure SQL Database.
fs: Arc-enabled SQL supports all supported SQL Server versions (2012 through current) and is primarily valued for governance, Entra authentication, and hybrid Kubernetes deployments—not as a workaround for unsupported versions.

q: What is Azure SQL Database in Microsoft Fabric, and when does it make more sense than a standalone Azure SQL Database?
a: Azure SQL Database in Microsoft Fabric is a fully managed relational database service built into the Fabric platform that exposes data natively through the OneLake logical data lake. Any table written to the SQL Database is automatically mirrored to OneLake in Delta Parquet format without a separate pipeline, enabling zero-ETL analytics in Fabric notebooks, Spark, and Power BI. You choose it over a standalone Azure SQL Database when your workload is already centred in Fabric—for example, a Lakehouse medalion architecture where the serving layer is transactional—because it eliminates the integration layer between OLTP and analytics. It also inherits Fabric's unified security model (workspace roles, Microsoft Purview) and billing. Standalone Azure SQL Database remains appropriate when you need fine-grained network isolation (private endpoints, VNet rules) or feature sets not yet available in Fabric SQL (e.g., advanced geo-replication configurations).
fj: Azure SQL Database in Microsoft Fabric is just a rebranding of Azure Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool.
fs: Fabric SQL Database is a fully operational OLTP engine (row store, full T-SQL DML/DDL) with automatic OneLake mirroring—completely different from Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool, which is a distributed columnar MPP warehouse.

q: Walk through deploying Azure SQL Managed Instance using the Azure CLI, including the required prerequisites and key parameters.
a: Before deploying SQL MI you need: a resource group, a dedicated subnet (minimum /27 CIDR, no NSG rules blocking required ports), and a route table attached to that subnet. The subnet cannot contain other resources and must be delegated to Microsoft.Sql/managedInstances. With those ready, you run `az sql mi create` specifying resource group, name, location, admin credentials, subnet resource ID, SKU (General Purpose or Business Critical), vCores count, and storage size in GB. Managed Instance creation takes 4–6 hours for a new cluster and ~minutes for an add-instance to an existing cluster. After creation, you retrieve the private FQDN (`<name>.public.<dns-zone>.database.windows.net` for public endpoint) and configure clients. For production, also set a maintenance window, enable Entra-only auth, and attach to an existing DNS zone if using custom DNS. The Business Critical tier automatically provisions three HA replicas in the same region.
fj: SQL Managed Instance can be deployed into any existing subnet in a VNet that already hosts other Azure resources.
fs: SQL MI requires a dedicated, delegated subnet. Sharing the subnet with other resources (VMs, App Service, etc.) is not supported and will cause deployment failure.

code.label: Deploy SQL Managed Instance via Azure CLI

q: How does table partitioning improve query performance and manageability in Azure SQL, and how do you implement it?
a: Table partitioning divides a large table into smaller, physically distinct units (partitions) stored across one or more filegroups, controlled by a partition function and partition scheme. Partition elimination lets the query optimizer skip entire partitions whose range does not satisfy a WHERE predicate, dramatically reducing I/O for range-based queries (e.g., filtering by date year). Manageability benefits include partition switching for near-instantaneous data loads and archival (moving an entire partition to a staging table in metadata-only time), and easier index maintenance by rebuilding one partition at a time during maintenance windows. To implement: (1) create a partition function defining the boundary values, (2) create a partition scheme mapping partitions to filegroups, (3) create or rebuild the clustered index ON that partition scheme. For Azure SQL Database, only PRIMARY filegroup is available, but partitioning still gives elimination and switch benefits.
fj: Partition switching requires copying all rows from one table to another, so it offers no performance advantage over DELETE/INSERT.
fs: Partition switching is a metadata-only operation when both tables share the same partition scheme and filegroup. No data movement occurs, making it nearly instantaneous regardless of row count.

code.label: Create partition function, scheme, and partitioned table
lines: -- 2. Partition scheme (Azure SQL: all on PRIMARY)
-- 3. Partitioned clustered index on existing table
ALTER TABLE dbo.Orders SWITCH PARTITION 1 TO dbo.Orders_Archive PARTITION 1;

q: Compare online and offline migration strategies to Azure SQL, including which tools support each mode and when you would choose one over the other.
a: An offline migration requires taking the source database offline (or accepting a static snapshot), moving the data, and only cutting over when migration is complete—meaning full application downtime from start to cutover. An online migration keeps the source live, performs an initial bulk load, then continuously replicates incremental changes (log-based CDC or log shipping), so downtime is only the final cutover window (seconds to minutes). Use offline when: the database is small, there is a maintenance window, or compliance forbids continuous replication. Use online when: the database is large, 24/7 SLA requirements exist, or business impact of downtime is unacceptable. The primary tool for both modes is Azure Database Migration Service (DMS) with the Azure SQL migration extension (now VS Code-based after Azure Data Studio retired in Feb 2026). BACPAC export/import via Azure Portal is offline-only and suited only for small databases (it locks the schema). Azure SQL Managed Instance also supports native backup-file-based migration to Blob Storage for offline scenarios.
fj: BACPAC import is recommended for migrating large production databases because it supports parallelism across multiple files.
fs: BACPAC is a logical export (schema + data as XML) that does not parallelize well and is best for small databases or dev/test. For large production migrations, DMS with online mode minimises downtime and is the Microsoft-recommended path.

q: What is the Azure SQL Managed Instance database Copy and Move feature, and how does it differ from backup/restore?
a: The database Copy and Move feature in SQL Managed Instance allows you to copy or move a database to another Managed Instance (within the same or a different subscription and region) as an online operation with no downtime. It uses distributed availability group technology under the hood—seeding the database to the target while both source and target remain online—then performs a near-instantaneous switchover. Copy keeps the source database operational and creates an independent copy on the target; Move removes the source after cutover. This differs from backup/restore in three key ways: (1) no manual backup files or Blob Storage required, (2) the operation is online with minimal impact on the source, and (3) the operation is orchestrated via the Azure portal, Azure CLI, or REST API with progress tracking. Use Copy/Move for cross-subscription scaling, cross-region DR setup, or consolidating instances, rather than for point-in-time recovery (which still requires backup/restore).
fj: Database Copy/Move in SQL MI creates a BACPAC file in Azure Blob Storage and then restores it on the target instance.
fs: Copy/Move uses distributed availability group seeding—a live replication mechanism—and never creates a BACPAC file. BACPAC is a logical backup format, while Copy/Move is a physical, online data movement operation.

q: What are the most common root causes of a failed migration to Azure SQL, and how do you diagnose and resolve each?
a: The most frequent migration failures fall into five categories. (1) Schema incompatibilities: features not supported in the target tier (e.g., cross-database queries in Azure SQL Database, SQL Server Agent in SQL DB vs. MI). Diagnose with Database Experimentation Assistant (DEA) or the Azure SQL migration extension assessment report before migrating. (2) Login/security mismatches: Windows logins and SQL Agent proxies do not migrate directly. Re-create as Entra identities or SQL logins on the target. (3) Network/firewall issues: DMS requires connectivity to both source and target; missing firewall rules or NSG blocks cause the migration to stall at data load. (4) Insufficient target size: undersized vCores or storage causes throttling. Monitor DMS activity logs and scale target before retry. (5) Transaction log growth on source: heavy write workload during online migration can outpace DMS log reader; reduce workload or schedule during off-peak. Always check DMS activity logs and Azure Monitor metrics, and run a test migration on a non-production copy before executing on production.
fj: If a DMS migration fails, the recommended fix is always to restart the migration from the beginning with a fresh full backup.
fs: DMS supports resuming from a checkpoint for online migrations; restarting from scratch is only necessary if the schema or connectivity issue corrupted the migration job state. Checking activity logs first often reveals a targeted fix that avoids a full restart.

q: How do you configure Microsoft Entra ID authentication for Azure SQL Database, and what are the two supported Entra auth modes?
a: Entra ID authentication for Azure SQL Database is configured at two levels. First, set an Entra admin on the logical server (a user or group) via the Azure Portal under 'Microsoft Entra admin' or via CLI/PowerShell—this is the server-level Entra administrator. Second, optionally enable Entra-only authentication, which disables SQL authentication entirely and forces all connections to use Entra tokens. The two modes are: (1) Mixed mode—both SQL logins and Entra identities are accepted; (2) Entra-only mode—SQL logins are disabled at the server level, enforcing a zero-trust posture. To create a database user from an Entra identity, connect as the Entra admin and run `CREATE USER [user@tenant.com] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER`. Managed identities (system-assigned or user-assigned) work the same way and are the recommended approach for app-to-database connections because they eliminate stored credentials. For SQL Managed Instance, the Entra admin is set per instance rather than logical server.
fj: Setting an Entra admin automatically disables SQL authentication for all databases on the server.
fs: Setting an Entra admin does not disable SQL authentication; it only adds an Entra-authenticated admin. You must explicitly enable Entra-only authentication to prevent SQL login usage.

code.label: Create Entra user and assign role in Azure SQL DB
lines: -- Run as Entra admin or sysadmin
-- Create user from Entra managed identity

q: How do you create contained database users from Microsoft Entra identities, and why are contained users preferred over server-level logins for Azure SQL Database?
a: Contained database users are created directly in the database without a corresponding server-level login using the `CREATE USER ... FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER` syntax. They are preferred in Azure SQL Database for several reasons: (1) Portability—the database carries its own user mappings, so it can be restored or copied to another logical server without orphaned-login issues; (2) Entra integration—Entra identities (users, groups, managed identities, service principals) can only be used as contained database users in Azure SQL DB; (3) Least privilege—granting access at the database level avoids unintended access to other databases on the same server. For groups, add the Entra group as a contained user and manage membership in Entra ID rather than maintaining per-user grants in SQL. This simplifies onboarding and offboarding: removing a user from the Entra group automatically revokes database access without a T-SQL change.
fj: Contained database users in Azure SQL DB still require a server-level login to exist first.
fs: Contained users are explicitly designed to work without a server-level login. The 'FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER' clause tells SQL to resolve the identity against Entra ID at authentication time, bypassing the login layer entirely.

code.label: Contained user from Entra group
lines: -- Connect to target database as Entra admin
-- Add Entra group as contained database user
WHERE type IN ('E','X');  -- E=External user, X=External group

q: How do you configure database and object-level permissions in Azure SQL using graphical tools, and what is the permission hierarchy?
a: SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) and Azure Data Studio provide graphical permission dialogs. In SSMS: right-click the database or object, select Properties > Permissions, add the principal, then check Grant/With Grant/Deny for each permission. The SQL Server permission hierarchy is: server (instance) → database → schema → object. Server permissions (e.g., VIEW SERVER STATE) apply only to SQL MI/SQL on VMs; Azure SQL Database uses database-scoped equivalents (VIEW DATABASE STATE). Key database-level permissions include CONNECT, CREATE TABLE, and EXECUTE. Object-level permissions (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, EXECUTE on a specific table or procedure) are the most granular. GRANT WITH GRANT OPTION lets the grantee re-grant the permission. DENY always overrides GRANT at the same scope. When troubleshooting effective permissions, use `fn_my_permissions` or the 'Effective Permissions' tab in SSMS Object Properties to see the resolved permission set for a principal.
fj: DENY on an object permission cannot be overridden by a GRANT at the schema or database level.
fs: DENY does override GRANT at the same or lower scope, but a higher-scope GRANT (e.g., CONTROL on the database) does not override a lower-scope DENY on an object. Permission resolution: membership in db_owner or CONTROL overrides most denies, but explicit DENY on the same securabe wins over a role-based GRANT at the same level.

q: What does the principle of least privilege mean in Azure SQL, and what specific mechanisms enforce it?
a: Least privilege means granting each principal only the minimum permissions required to perform their job function, and nothing more. In Azure SQL this is enforced through: (1) Fixed database roles—use `db_datareader`/`db_datawriter` instead of `db_owner`; (2) Object-level GRANT instead of schema-level GRANT when a user needs access to only one table; (3) Stored procedure ownership chaining—expose data manipulation through signed procs rather than direct table SELECT; (4) Row-level security policies to restrict which rows a user sees even within a table they can query; (5) Dynamic data masking to obscure column values from non-privileged roles; (6) Schema separation—place sensitive tables in a restricted schema and grant EXECUTE on schema rather than individual objects. Avoid the db_owner role for application accounts; use custom roles with only EXECUTE on needed procedures. Audit permissions periodically using `sys.database_permissions` and the SSMS 'Effective Permissions' view.
fj: Granting a user the db_datawriter role is sufficient for least privilege when the application only needs to write data.
fs: db_datawriter grants INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE on every table in the database. Least privilege would grant only the specific DML operations on only the tables the application actually writes to, not a blanket role across all tables.

q: How does Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) work in Azure SQL, what is the difference between service-managed and customer-managed keys, and how do you enable it?
a: TDE encrypts the entire database at rest by encrypting data files, log files, and backups using a Database Encryption Key (DEK) protected by a TDE protector. IO operations are encrypted and decrypted transparently at the page level with no application changes required. Service-managed TDE (the default for all new Azure SQL databases) uses a Microsoft-managed key stored in Azure Key Vault; the customer never handles key material. Customer-managed TDE (Bring Your Own Key, BYOK) lets you store the TDE protector in your own Azure Key Vault, giving you control over key rotation, revocation, and HSM backing. Revoking the key immediately renders the database inaccessible. For Azure SQL DB, TDE is ON by default and cannot be disabled unless you explicitly turn it off (not recommended). To enable on an existing server or check status: use `ALTER DATABASE [db] SET ENCRYPTION ON` in T-SQL, or toggle via Portal/CLI. BYOK requires granting the server's managed identity GET, WRAPKEY, and UNWRAPKEY access to the Key Vault key.
fj: TDE in Azure SQL DB encrypts only the data files; transaction log files are not encrypted.
fs: TDE encrypts data files, transaction log files, and all backups (full, differential, and log). This ensures no sensitive data is readable if physical media is stolen.

code.label: Enable TDE and check encryption state
lines: ALTER DATABASE SalesDB SET ENCRYPTION ON;
JOIN sys.databases db ON d.database_id = db.database_id

q: What is column-level encryption in Azure SQL and how does it differ from TDE?
a: Column-level (object-level) encryption in Azure SQL uses the `ENCRYPTBYKEY` / `DECRYPTBYKEY` T-SQL functions together with symmetric keys protected by a certificate or asymmetric key. Unlike TDE, which encrypts the entire database at rest transparently, column encryption requires explicit application or query changes: you must call encryption/decryption functions in your SELECT/INSERT statements, and the encrypted value is stored as `varbinary`. This means the application (or a privileged DBA with the key) must decrypt values explicitly—non-privileged users see binary blobs. Use cases include protecting a specific high-sensitivity column (SSN, credit card) from DBAs who otherwise have full database access. However, for strong protection against DBAs, Always Encrypted is preferred because the encryption/decryption happens client-side and the server never sees plaintext. Column-level encryption via `ENCRYPTBYKEY` is simpler to implement but weaker in terms of DBA isolation.
fj: TDE and column-level encryption are equivalent in protection level; the only difference is TDE protects the whole database while column encryption targets specific columns.
fs: They differ significantly in trust boundaries. TDE protects against offline media theft but DBAs with server access can read all plaintext. Column-level encryption requires a decryption key even for DBAs, but the key is still accessible server-side. Always Encrypted goes further by keeping keys on the client only.

q: How do server-level and database-level firewall rules work in Azure SQL Database, and when would you use private endpoints instead?
a: Server-level firewall rules are stored in the `master` database and apply to all databases on the logical server; they are configured via Portal, CLI, or T-SQL (only when connected as server admin). Database-level rules are stored in individual databases and apply only to that database, configurable via T-SQL `sp_set_database_firewall_rule`—useful for multi-tenant scenarios where each tenant's IP is stored per-database. Azure also provides the 'Allow Azure services and resources to access this server' toggle (a special rule for 0.0.0.0), which lets any Azure-resident service connect—this is convenient but broadens the attack surface. For production workloads with sensitive data, private endpoints are preferred: they place a private IP inside your VNet so traffic never leaves the Azure backbone, firewall rules become irrelevant, and you control access via NSGs and DNS. VNet service endpoints are a middle ground: traffic stays on Azure backbone but the server still has a public endpoint (just restricted to specific subnets).
fj: Database-level firewall rules override server-level rules, so setting a database-level DENY can block access even if a server-level ALLOW rule exists.
fs: Firewall rules are ALLOW-only; there are no DENY rules at either level. A connection is allowed if it matches any applicable rule—database-level OR server-level. They do not override each other; the server checks both, and the most permissive match wins.

code.label: Manage firewall rules via T-SQL
lines: -- Server-level rule (run in master as server admin)

q: Explain Always Encrypted and Always Encrypted with VBS enclaves—what problem each solves, how they differ, and when to choose enclaves.
a: Always Encrypted protects sensitive column data by encrypting it client-side using a Column Master Key (CMK) stored outside SQL Server (Azure Key Vault, Windows Certificate Store). The database engine never sees plaintext—it stores, retrieves, and replicates ciphertext only. This protects data even from DBAs with full server access. The limitation of standard Always Encrypted is that queries on encrypted columns can only use equality lookups on deterministically-encrypted columns; range queries, LIKE, and joins on encrypted columns are impossible without decrypting client-side first. Always Encrypted with Secure Enclaves solves this by running query processing inside a trusted execution environment (TEE) where plaintext is briefly available only inside the enclave—inaccessible to the OS, hypervisor, or DBA. Azure SQL Database supports two enclave types: SGX (hardware-based, available on specific hardware) and VBS (Virtualization-Based Security, software-based, available broadly via Hyper-V isolated virtual secure mode). VBS is recommended when SGX hardware is unavailable; it supports rich computations (LIKE, range queries, pattern matching) on encrypted columns while maintaining the DBA-isolation guarantee. Choose enclaves when you need both DBA protection and rich query predicates on encrypted columns.
fj: Always Encrypted with enclaves eliminates the need to store a Column Master Key outside SQL Server because the enclave provides all necessary key protection.
fs: The CMK must still be stored outside SQL Server (e.g., Azure Key Vault) regardless of enclave type. The enclave only provides a secure computation environment for query processing on encrypted data; key custody remains with the client or Key Vault.

code.label: Create enclave-enabled column master key and encrypted column
lines: -- Requires SSMS Always Encrypted wizard or PowerShell
-- T-SQL metadata after wizard creates the CMK/CEK:
-- Query on encrypted column using enclave (equality + LIKE)
WHERE SSN LIKE '%-1234';  -- Only works with enclave-enabled CEK

q: How do you apply a data classification strategy in Azure SQL, and what Azure service integrates with it for compliance reporting?
a: Data classification in Azure SQL uses the Information Protection framework built into the database engine (accessible in SSMS via right-click > Tasks > Classify Data, or via T-SQL `ADD SENSITIVITY CLASSIFICATION` statements). Each column is tagged with a Sensitivity Label (e.g., Confidential, Highly Confidential) and an Information Type (e.g., Financial, Health, Credentials). These metadata labels persist in database metadata (`sys.sensitivity_classifications`) and are surfaced in Azure Defender for SQL (part of Microsoft Defender for Cloud), which generates compliance reports showing how many columns contain sensitive data and whether they are adequately protected (auditing on, masking applied, etc.). The classification also integrates with Microsoft Purview for data governance across the estate. Best practice: classify during initial schema design, automate with Purview's data scan to discover new columns, and review classifications during quarterly security audits. Labels do not enforce access controls by themselves—they are metadata that drives policy and alerting.
fj: Adding a sensitivity classification to a column automatically applies dynamic data masking to that column.
fs: Classification is metadata only; it does not apply any access controls or masking automatically. You must separately configure dynamic data masking, RLS, or Always Encrypted. Classification informs people and tools about what protection should be applied.

code.label: Add and query sensitivity classifications

q: How do you configure auditing for an Azure SQL Database server and database, and what are the differences between server-level and database-level audit policies?
a: Auditing in Azure SQL captures database events and writes them to an Azure Storage account, Log Analytics workspace, or Event Hub. Server-level auditing is configured on the logical server and applies to all databases on that server, including new databases created in the future. Database-level auditing applies only to the specific database and can supplement or override server audit settings. If both are configured, the database-level log and server-level log are written independently. In the Portal: Security > Auditing. Key settings include: storage retention period (90 days minimum recommended), audit action groups (SUCCESSFUL_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP, DATABASE_OBJECT_CHANGE_GROUP, etc.), and whether to audit failed logins. Audit logs are queryable via `sys.fn_get_audit_file` in T-SQL or through Azure Monitor Log Analytics. Microsoft Defender for SQL Advanced Threat Protection also uses the audit logs to detect anomalous access patterns. Enable server-level auditing for baseline compliance, and add database-level auditing for sensitive databases that require finer action-group control.
fj: Configuring a database-level audit policy disables the server-level audit policy for that database.
fs: Database-level auditing does not disable server-level auditing; both run in parallel and write to their respective targets. A database inherits the server policy and can also add its own, resulting in two separate audit log streams.

q: What is Change Data Capture (CDC) in Azure SQL and how does it differ from Change Tracking?
a: Change Data Capture (CDC) reads the SQL Server transaction log and records row-level INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE changes in dedicated change tables, capturing both the old and new column values. It is suited for ETL pipelines and audit trails that need full change history with before/after values. Change Tracking is a lighter-weight mechanism that only records which rows changed and in which direction (net change per row since a given version), without storing old values—suitable for synchronization scenarios where you only need to know what to re-sync, not what it was before. Key differences: CDC writes change data to the database (extra storage); Change Tracking stores minimal metadata. CDC requires the SQL Server Agent job (or an Azure-managed background process in Azure SQL) to process the log. To enable CDC in Azure SQL: `sys.sp_cdc_enable_db` at database level, then `sys.sp_cdc_enable_table` per table. Change Tracking is enabled via `ALTER DATABASE ... SET CHANGE_TRACKING = ON`. Both support full table or column subset tracking.
fj: Change Tracking captures before and after values for every column, making it equivalent to CDC for audit purposes.
fs: Change Tracking only stores the primary key and net change type (insert/update/delete) per row since a given synchronization version. It does not capture before or after column values—CDC does. For auditing or data replication requiring value history, CDC is required.

code.label: Enable CDC on database and table
lines: -- Enable CDC on a specific table

q: How does Dynamic Data Masking work in Azure SQL, and what masking functions are available?
a: Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) obfuscates sensitive column values in query results returned to non-privileged users, without changing the underlying stored data. It is policy-based and configured per column. Users with the UNMASK permission or membership in db_owner see real values; all others see the masked output. Available masking functions: (1) Default—masks strings with 'XXXX', numbers with 0, date types with '1900-01-01'; (2) Email—shows first letter and domain suffix (e.g., 'a@XXXX.com'); (3) Random—replaces numeric values with a random number within a specified range; (4) Custom String—exposes a configurable prefix and suffix with 'X' padding in the middle (e.g., 'CC-XXXX-1234' for card numbers). To add masking: `ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'default()')`. DDM is not an encryption mechanism—users with direct table access or the UNMASK privilege see plaintext. It is a presentation-layer control for protecting data from developers, analysts, or support staff who run ad-hoc queries.
fj: Dynamic Data Masking prevents the application layer from ever reading sensitive values, making it suitable as the sole protection for PII columns.
fs: DDM is a display-layer obfuscation tool only. Any user with UNMASK, SELECT with elevated permissions, or direct table access bypasses masking. For genuine encryption-level protection, combine DDM with Always Encrypted or column-level encryption.

code.label: Apply and test dynamic data masking
lines: -- Add email mask to Email column
ALTER COLUMN Email ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'email()');
-- Add custom string mask to credit card
ALTER COLUMN CardNumber ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'partial(0,"XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-",4)');

q: What is the Azure SQL Ledger feature, what types of ledger tables exist, and how do you verify tamper evidence?
a: Azure SQL Ledger provides cryptographic proof that data has not been tampered with. It uses a Merkle tree to hash each database transaction; these hashes form a blockchain-like chain of blocks stored in `sys.database_ledger_blocks`. Two table types: (1) Updatable Ledger Tables—allow INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on user-facing tables, with all historical row versions preserved in a separate ledger history table and the current state also hashed; (2) Append-Only Ledger Tables—only allow INSERT, making them ideal for audit trails (e.g., financial transaction logs) where records must never be altered. To verify integrity, generate a database digest (`sys.sp_generate_database_ledger_digest`) and store it externally (Azure Confidential Ledger, Azure Blob with immutability, or on-premises WORM storage). Later, run `sys.sp_verify_database_ledger` with the external digest to cryptographically confirm no rows were changed since that digest was captured. Any modification to ledger table rows or the digest chain will cause verification to fail with a specific error identifying the affected block.
fj: Append-only ledger tables allow soft-deletes by setting a 'deleted' flag column, which is why they are suitable for audit trails.
fs: Append-only ledger tables literally permit only INSERT operations at the engine level—UPDATE and DELETE are blocked with an error. They are suitable for audit trails precisely because even DBAs cannot alter or delete records. A 'deleted' flag pattern would not be needed because no record can be removed.

code.label: Create an append-only ledger table and verify integrity
lines: -- Generate a digest to store externally
-- Verify integrity using stored digest JSON

q: How do you implement Row-Level Security (RLS) in Azure SQL, and what are the two policy types?
a: Row-Level Security uses inline table-valued functions (predicates) attached to a security policy to filter or block row access based on the execution context. Two predicate types: (1) Filter predicates—silently filter rows from SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations (the user simply sees fewer rows, with no error); (2) Block predicates—prevent INSERT or UPDATE operations that would result in rows the user cannot see (AFTER INSERT, AFTER UPDATE, BEFORE UPDATE, BEFORE DELETE). Implementation steps: (1) create an inline TVF that returns 1 when the current user should see the row; (2) create a security policy binding the TVF to the table as a filter and/or block predicate; (3) mark the policy as ON. Performance impact: the predicate function is inlined into every query on the table. Use schema-binding on the TVF and ensure the predicate column is indexed. Bypass scenarios: db_owner and users with the CONTROL permission bypass all RLS by default unless you add a blocking predicate that includes those users.
fj: Row-Level Security raises an error when a user queries rows they are not permitted to see, similar to a permission denied error.
fs: Filter predicates silently exclude rows from result sets—no error is raised and the user cannot distinguish between a row that does not exist and a row they cannot see. Block predicates raise errors only for write operations (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) that would violate the policy.

code.label: Implement Row-Level Security by tenant
lines: -- Predicate function: user sees only their tenant's rows
ADD FILTER PREDICATE dbo.fn_TenantFilter(TenantID) ON dbo.Orders,
ADD BLOCK  PREDICATE dbo.fn_TenantFilter(TenantID) ON dbo.Orders AFTER INSERT
-- Set session context at app layer before queries

q: What is a performance baseline in Azure SQL and how do you establish one?
a: A performance baseline captures normal operating metrics so you can detect deviations that indicate problems. For Azure SQL, establish a baseline by collecting metrics over a representative workload period (typically a full business cycle: day, week, month). Key metrics to capture: CPU percentage, DTU/vCore utilisation, data IO percentage, log write percentage, storage used, active connections, deadlocks per second, and average query duration for top workloads. Tools: Azure Monitor metrics (portal/CLI/Log Analytics), Query Store (captures per-query execution statistics including avg and max duration, CPU, logical reads), and DMVs (`sys.dm_exec_query_stats`, `sys.dm_os_wait_stats`). Export Query Store reports to establish median and 95th-percentile latency per query. The baseline then anchors: alerting thresholds (alert when CPU > 85% sustained), post-change validation (compare before/after a schema change), and capacity planning (trend storage/DTU growth over months).
fj: A performance baseline only needs to capture peak-hour metrics because off-peak activity does not affect performance decisions.
fs: A complete baseline captures both peak and off-peak periods. Maintenance jobs (index rebuilds, backups, statistics updates) that run off-peak can still spike IO and CPU. Off-peak baselines also reveal whether read replicas or overnight ETL are degrading daytime workloads.

q: What are the primary sources of performance metrics for Azure SQL Database and how do you interpret them?
a: Azure SQL Database exposes metrics at multiple layers: (1) Azure Monitor / Portal metrics—DTU or vCore percentage, data IO %, log write %, workers %, sessions %, memory usage; these are aggregate resource utilisation at the database/elastic pool level and are the first place to check for resource pressure. (2) Query Store—`sys.query_store_runtime_stats`, `sys.query_store_query`, `sys.query_store_plan` expose per-query plan, execution count, average and total CPU/duration/logical reads. Use 'Top Resource Consuming Queries' report in SSMS. (3) DMVs—`sys.dm_exec_query_stats` for in-memory query stats (reset on restart), `sys.dm_os_wait_stats` for server-wide wait categories, `sys.dm_exec_requests` for active workloads. (4) Extended Events sessions—low-overhead event tracing for capturing specific event types (query completion, deadlocks, blocking). Interpretation: high CPU + high logical reads → query or index issue; high log write % → excessive writes or large transactions; high WRITELOG waits → log IO bottleneck; high LCK waits → blocking.
fj: sys.dm_exec_query_stats provides persistent query performance history across server restarts, making it the best source for long-term trend analysis.
fs: sys.dm_exec_query_stats data is in-memory and resets when the plan is evicted from cache or the server restarts. For persistent query history, use Query Store, which writes statistics to disk and survives restarts.

q: How do you configure and monitor activity and performance in Azure SQL Database using the Azure Portal and Query Store?
a: In the Azure Portal, the Monitoring section of a SQL Database resource exposes: Metrics (configurable charts for DTU %, CPU %, IO %, log %, connections), Diagnostic Settings (stream logs to Storage/Log Analytics/Event Hub), and Query Performance Insight (a portal UI over Query Store data showing top CPU/duration queries). To use Query Store directly, enable it with `ALTER DATABASE [db] SET QUERY_STORE = ON` (on by default in Azure SQL), then in SSMS: Reports > Query Store > Top Resource Consuming Queries, Regressed Queries, Query Wait Statistics. Key Query Store views: `sys.query_store_query` (query text hash), `sys.query_store_plan` (plan XML), `sys.query_store_runtime_stats` (aggregated execution metrics). For real-time activity: `sys.dm_exec_requests` (running requests), `sys.dm_exec_sessions` (active sessions), and Activity Monitor in SSMS. Set Azure Monitor metric alerts on CPU/DTU for proactive notification.
fj: Query Store must be manually enabled on every new Azure SQL Database before it will collect execution statistics.
fs: Query Store is enabled by default for all new databases in Azure SQL Database with READ_WRITE mode. It only needs manual enablement on databases migrated from SQL Server where it was disabled, or after an accidental `ALTER DATABASE ... SET QUERY_STORE = OFF`.

q: What is Azure SQL Database watcher and what advantages does it have over other monitoring tools?
a: Database watcher is a managed monitoring service (in preview as of 2026) that collects comprehensive diagnostic data from Azure SQL Database and Azure SQL Managed Instance at low latency and stores it in a time-series data store (Azure Data Explorer by default, or Fabric Real-Time Analytics). Advantages over other tools: (1) Sub-minute data collection latency compared to Azure Monitor metrics which have 1-minute minimum granularity; (2) Estate-level monitoring—a single watcher instance can monitor tens to hundreds of databases across subscriptions; (3) Rich query-level detail—captures active sessions, blocking, query plans, wait stats at collection time without requiring the user to set up Extended Events sessions; (4) Pre-built dashboards in Azure portal. To configure: create a database watcher resource, add SQL targets (databases or managed instances), grant the watcher's managed identity the `##MS_DatabaseWatcherAdmin##` server role on each target, and start the watcher. Data is streamed to the connected data store for querying and dashboarding.
fj: Database watcher replaces Query Store as the primary mechanism for capturing query execution plans in Azure SQL Database.
fs: Database watcher and Query Store serve different purposes. Query Store is engine-native persistent execution statistics tied to plan management (force plans, regression detection). Database watcher is an external monitoring collector for operational observability across an estate. They complement each other.

q: How do you identify and resolve session blocking in Azure SQL Database?
a: Session blocking occurs when one transaction holds a lock that another transaction is waiting for. Identify blocking using: (1) `sys.dm_exec_requests`—look for `blocking_session_id > 0`; (2) `sys.dm_os_waiting_tasks`—shows wait type (LCK_M_X, LCK_M_S, etc.) and the blocking session; (3) `sys.dm_tran_locks`—shows all active locks and waiters; (4) Extended Events `blocked_process_report` event (enable with blocked process threshold > 0 in seconds). To investigate: get the blocking session's current statement via `sys.dm_exec_sql_text(r.sql_handle)` and its open transaction age via `sys.dm_tran_session_transactions`. Resolution strategies: (1) Optimise the blocking query to hold locks for shorter time (reduce transaction scope, add appropriate indexes); (2) Use READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT isolation (RCSI) to eliminate read/write blocking by giving readers a snapshot; (3) For deadlocks (circular blocking), query Store or Extended Events deadlock graph events, then adjust transaction ordering or use short transactions; (4) Kill the blocking session with `KILL <spid>` as emergency relief. Set `blocking_session_report` threshold to alert proactively.
fj: Enabling READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT prevents all forms of blocking including deadlocks.
fs: RCSI eliminates reader/writer blocking by giving readers a consistent snapshot without acquiring shared locks. However, it does not prevent writer/writer deadlocks—two transactions competing for X locks in opposing order will still deadlock. Deadlock prevention requires proper transaction design (consistent lock ordering, shorter transactions).

code.label: Identify blocking chain and blocking query text

q: Which DMVs are most useful for identifying performance issues in Azure SQL Database, and what does each reveal?
a: Key DMVs for performance diagnostics: (1) `sys.dm_exec_requests`—currently executing requests with CPU time, reads, writes, blocking session, wait type; (2) `sys.dm_exec_query_stats`—aggregated execution statistics per cached query plan (total/avg CPU, elapsed time, logical reads) since plan was compiled or instance restarted; (3) `sys.dm_os_wait_stats`—server-level aggregated wait counts by category; high PAGEIOLATCH = IO pressure, WRITELOG = log IO, CXPACKET = parallelism, LCK = locking; (4) `sys.dm_exec_session_wait_stats`—per-session waits since session started; (5) `sys.dm_index_usage_stats`—seek/scan/lookup/update counts per index since restart—identifies unused indexes and missing seek coverage; (6) `sys.dm_db_missing_index_details` / `sys.dm_db_missing_index_group_stats`—query optimizer's suggestions for missing indexes with impact score; (7) `sys.dm_tran_active_transactions`—active transaction age. Combine `dm_exec_requests` with `dm_exec_sql_text` and `dm_exec_query_plan` (cross apply) to get statement text and XML plan for a running query.
fj: sys.dm_db_missing_index_details provides a guaranteed list of indexes that will improve performance and should always be created as reported.
fs: Missing index DMVs are hints from the query optimizer for specific queries it has seen, not a tested recommendation. Creating every suggested index increases write overhead, storage, and maintenance cost. Always evaluate impact score, redundancy with existing indexes, and test in a non-production environment first.

code.label: Top queries by total CPU using DMVs

q: How do you identify and implement index changes to improve query performance in Azure SQL?
a: Start with Query Store 'Top Resource Consuming Queries' or `sys.dm_exec_query_stats` to find high-cost queries. For each query, review the actual execution plan (SSMS: include Actual Execution Plan, or `sys.dm_exec_query_plan`). Look for: Table Scan or Clustered Index Scan operators where a seek is possible (missing index), Key Lookup operators (covering index needed), High Estimated vs. Actual row count (stale statistics), and Sort operators with high cost (missing ordered index). Cross-check with `sys.dm_db_missing_index_group_stats` for optimizer hints and `sys.dm_index_usage_stats` for low-use indexes to drop. When adding an index: create nonclustered index with key columns matching the WHERE predicate, then include columns needed by SELECT to cover the query without a lookup. Test impact using SET STATISTICS IO, TIME ON and compare logical reads before and after. Drop duplicate or unused indexes to reduce write amplification. For Azure SQL Database, automatic index tuning can create and test indexes automatically if configured.
fj: A covering index must include all columns of the table as INCLUDE columns to be effective.
fs: A covering index only needs to include the columns referenced in the SELECT list that are not already in the key—not all columns. Including unnecessary columns wastes index space and increases maintenance cost. The goal is to satisfy the specific query without a Key Lookup, not to replicate the entire row.

code.label: Create covering nonclustered index to eliminate Key Lookup
lines: -- Original query causing Key Lookup on CIX_Orders_OrderID
-- SELECT OrderDate, CustomerID FROM dbo.Orders WHERE Status = 'Open'
-- Add covering index: key = Status, include = OrderDate, CustomerID
SELECT OrderDate, CustomerID FROM dbo.Orders WHERE Status = 'Open';

q: How do you read an execution plan in SQL Server / Azure SQL and what are the most important operators and properties to examine?
a: An execution plan is the query optimizer's chosen set of operators (tree of steps) to retrieve data. Key elements to examine: (1) Cost percentage on each operator—high cost on a single operator (especially Nested Loops or Hash Match) indicates the bottleneck; (2) Estimated vs. Actual rows—large discrepancy means stale or missing statistics, which can force wrong plan choices; (3) Operator types—Table/Index Scan vs. Index Seek (seek is better; scan reads all rows); (4) Key Lookup—an extra operation to retrieve non-indexed columns from the clustered index; usually fixed by adding INCLUDE columns to the nonclustered index; (5) Warning symbols—yellow triangle indicates missing statistics, unmatched data types (implicit conversion), or spill to tempdb; (6) Sort operator with high cost—can be resolved by adding an indexed order; (7) Parallelism (Distribute Streams, Gather Streams)—check degree of parallelism and whether MAXDOP limiting is appropriate. To get the plan: `SET STATISTICS XML ON` or `sys.dm_exec_query_plan` for cached plans; use SSMS graphical plan for visual analysis.
fj: A low-cost clustered index scan on a large table is always acceptable because clustered scans use sequential IO.
fs: Sequential IO is more efficient than random IO, but a full clustered index scan on a large table can still consume enormous IO and memory. If a predicate is present, an appropriate nonclustered index seek + key lookup (or covering index) will almost always outperform a full scan. Always compare actual logical reads, not just cost estimates.

q: What is Intelligent Insights in Azure SQL and what performance patterns does it detect?
a: Intelligent Insights is a built-in Azure SQL feature that uses machine learning to continuously compare current query workload performance against a 7-day historical baseline and automatically detect degradations. When a degradation is detected, a diagnostic log entry is generated with: a root cause category, affected queries, metrics showing the degradation magnitude, and recommended actions. Detected patterns include: CPU reaching limits, data IO limits, log write limits, high memory consumption, lock waits exceeding baseline, database max session/worker limit reached, tempdb contention, long transaction hold time, query plan regression (plan changed and is now slower), query regression (query started running slower without plan change), and waiting on index creation/rebuild. Intelligent Insights outputs to Azure Monitor Diagnostics (SQLInsights log), Azure Storage, or Event Hub. While SQL Insights (the Azure Monitor Workbook experience) was retired, Intelligent Insights as a diagnostic log stream remains active. Review using Log Analytics queries against the AzureDiagnostics table, filtering for Category = 'SQLInsights'.
fj: Intelligent Insights can only detect performance issues caused by missing indexes and will not report locking or resource limit problems.
fs: Intelligent Insights detects a wide range of patterns including locking, session/worker limits, tempdb contention, memory pressure, and query plan regression—not just missing indexes. The feature uses ML comparison against baseline rather than rule-based checks for a specific set of issues.

q: How do you implement index maintenance and statistics update tasks in Azure SQL Database?
a: Index fragmentation degrades IO efficiency; statistics staleness causes poor cardinality estimates and suboptimal plans. In Azure SQL Database, SQL Server Agent is not available; schedule maintenance using Elastic Jobs, Azure Automation runbooks, or Azure Functions on a timer trigger. Strategies: for index fragmentation, check `sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats` for average_fragmentation_in_percent; rebuild (ALTER INDEX ... REBUILD) when > 30%, reorganise (ALTER INDEX ... REORGANIZE, online, less blocking) when 10-30%, leave alone when < 10%. For statistics, use `UPDATE STATISTICS` or rebuild indexes with STATISTICS_NORECOMPUTE = OFF. Azure SQL has auto-update statistics enabled by default (triggered at 20% row change threshold, lower for large tables). For large tables, consider AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS_ASYNC = ON to update stats in a background thread without blocking queries. Ola Hallengren's maintenance scripts are widely adapted for Azure SQL Elastic Jobs. Schedule weekly rebuilds for heavily-modified tables, nightly REORGANIZE for moderately fragmented ones.
fj: Rebuilding an index always requires taking the table offline, causing significant downtime for large tables.
fs: Online index rebuilds (ALTER INDEX ... REBUILD WITH (ONLINE = ON)) are available in Azure SQL Database (Standard/Premium/General Purpose and above) and allow the table to remain accessible for reads and writes during the rebuild. Only the brief final phase requires a schema stability lock.

code.label: Check fragmentation and rebuild/reorganize indexes
lines: ALTER INDEX IX_Orders_OrderDate ON dbo.Orders REBUILD WITH (ONLINE = ON);
ALTER INDEX IX_Orders_Status ON dbo.Orders REORGANIZE;

q: How do you implement database integrity checks in Azure SQL, and how do you handle DBCC CHECKDB in a managed Azure environment?
a: DBCC CHECKDB verifies the logical and physical integrity of all objects in a database, detecting page corruption, allocation errors, and structural inconsistencies. In Azure SQL Database, Microsoft runs DBCC CHECKDB internally and automatically; you can also run it manually. Azure SQL MI supports manual DBCC CHECKDB execution. For large databases, run `DBCC CHECKDB WITH PHYSICAL_ONLY` first—it checks physical page structure and allocation consistency without checking logical cross-row relationships, completing much faster and catching most hardware-caused corruption. Follow with a full `DBCC CHECKDB` during a maintenance window for complete validation. In Azure SQL Database, because storage is Azure-managed with replication, the risk of physical media corruption is very low, but logical corruption from application bugs still occurs. Integrity check outputs go to SQL Server error log and SSMS Messages tab; errors are reported with error number and object context. For auto-repair: `DBCC CHECKDB WITH REPAIR_REBUILD` fixes non-data-loss-risk errors; `REPAIR_ALLOW_DATA_LOSS` fixes critical errors but may delete pages (last resort—restore from backup instead).
fj: DBCC CHECKDB in Azure SQL Database must be run by Microsoft support only; customers cannot execute it directly.
fs: Customers can run DBCC CHECKDB directly in Azure SQL Database (and full access in SQL MI). In Azure SQL Database, Microsoft also runs it internally, but that does not prevent or require customers from running it for their own validation purposes.

q: How does automatic tuning work in Azure SQL Database, and what are the three tuning actions it can perform?
a: Automatic tuning in Azure SQL Database uses built-in intelligence to continuously monitor query performance using Query Store history and applies tuning actions with validation. Three tuning actions: (1) FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN—when a query plan regression is detected (a new plan is performing worse than a previous plan), SQL automatically forces the last known good plan. This is enabled by default and applies within minutes of detecting the regression. (2) CREATE_INDEX—identifies queries that would benefit from a new nonclustered index based on execution plan analysis, creates the index, measures actual improvement, and drops it if the performance gain is insufficient. (3) DROP_INDEX—identifies unused or duplicate indexes that consume maintenance resources and space, drops them after confirming they are not needed. Actions 2 and 3 are not enabled by default and must be explicitly turned on. Configure via Portal (Automatic tuning blade) or T-SQL `ALTER DATABASE ... SET AUTOMATIC_TUNING (FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN = ON, CREATE_INDEX = ON)`. All automatic actions are logged in `sys.dm_db_tuning_recommendations` with outcome status.
fj: Automatic tuning replaces Query Store; once enabled, Query Store is disabled to avoid conflicts.
fs: Automatic tuning is powered by Query Store—it requires Query Store to be enabled in READ_WRITE mode. Enabling automatic tuning does not disable Query Store; rather, it adds an ML layer on top of Query Store's execution history.

code.label: Enable automatic tuning and review recommendations
lines: -- Review tuning recommendations and their outcomes

q: How do you use SQL Server Agent in Azure SQL Managed Instance to schedule regular maintenance jobs?
a: SQL Server Agent is available in Azure SQL Managed Instance (not in Azure SQL Database). To schedule maintenance: (1) In SSMS Object Explorer, expand SQL Server Agent > Jobs > New Job; (2) Define Job Steps (T-SQL, SSIS package, PowerShell, or cmdexec); (3) In Schedules, create a recurring schedule (daily at 2 AM for backups, weekly Sunday for index rebuilds); (4) Set notifications on the Notifications page (email/pager on failure via Database Mail and an Operator). Database Mail must be configured first with an SMTP profile and operator email. Alert the Operations team using SQL Agent Alerts tied to SQL Server error severity levels or specific error numbers. Multi-server administration can target jobs across multiple MI instances using a Master/Target (MSX/TSX) server model, although this requires network connectivity between instances. Monitor job history via `msdb.dbo.sysjobhistory` and `Agent > Job Activity Monitor` in SSMS. For Azure SQL Database, replace SQL Agent with Elastic Jobs (the platform's native scheduler).
fj: SQL Server Agent in SQL MI stores its job metadata in the master database.
fs: SQL Server Agent stores all job definitions, schedules, history, and operators in the msdb system database, not master. Backing up and restoring msdb is how you migrate Agent jobs between instances.

q: How do you configure job alerts and notifications in SQL Server Agent on Azure SQL Managed Instance?
a: Notifications in SQL Server Agent require three components: (1) Database Mail—configure an SMTP mail profile via `msdb.dbo.sysmail_add_account_sp` and profile procedures, then enable Database Mail in sp_configure; (2) Operator—create an operator in SQL Agent > Operators with an email address (this is the recipient); (3) Alert or Job Notification—either attach a notification to a Job (on failure/success/completion) pointing to the operator, or create an Agent Alert triggered by SQL Server error severity ≥ 16, a specific error number, or a WMI condition. For proactive operational monitoring, create alerts for severity 17 (insufficient resources), 19 (resource error), and 24/25 (hardware/fatal errors). In Azure SQL MI, alerts and notifications work the same as SQL Server on-premises. For additional Azure-native alerting, use Azure Monitor Metric Alerts on the MI resource (CPU, storage) which do not require Database Mail and route to Action Groups (email, SMS, webhook, ITSM).
fj: SQL Agent notifications work without configuring Database Mail if you use the 'Net send' notification method.
fs: Net send notifications are deprecated and unavailable in modern Windows Server versions and Azure SQL MI. Email via Database Mail is the supported method for SQL Agent notifications in contemporary environments.

q: How do you troubleshoot a failing SQL Server Agent job in Azure SQL Managed Instance?
a: Start with the Job Activity Monitor (SSMS > SQL Server Agent > Job Activity Monitor) to see last run status and duration. For failures: (1) Check Job History—right-click the job > View History, expand the failed step, read the Message field which contains the error text, step exit code, and T-SQL error number; (2) Check SQL Server Error Log (`sys.sp_readerrorlog` or SSMS > Management > SQL Server Logs) for error severity ≥ 16 events at the time of the job run; (3) For permission errors, verify the job step owner and proxy account have the required permissions in the target database; (4) For network/external steps, verify connectivity from the MI to the target (Linked Server, SMTP server); (5) Query `msdb.dbo.sysjobhistory` directly for programmatic analysis across multiple jobs; (6) Check if SQL Agent service is running via sys.dm_server_services or Azure Portal MI diagnostics. Common pitfalls: job step runs under a different security context than testing (use a proxy), implicit transactions left open in a prior step blocking the next step, and job schedule timezone mismatch (MI uses UTC).
fj: If a SQL Agent job fails, restarting the SQL Agent service will clear the error and allow the job to succeed.
fs: Restarting SQL Agent clears the in-memory job queue but does not fix the underlying error. The job will fail again on the next scheduled run. The correct approach is to diagnose the error from job history, fix the root cause (permission, code, connectivity), and then run the job manually to verify the fix.

q: How do you monitor and troubleshoot Azure SQL deployment issues using Azure Monitor and activity logs?
a: Azure deployment issues for SQL resources are diagnosed through: (1) Azure Activity Log—records all control-plane operations (create, update, delete, failover) for SQL databases, servers, and managed instances; filter by resource type and time range; failed deployments show error codes and messages; (2) Azure Resource Manager deployment logs—in the resource group's Deployments blade, each ARM/Bicep deployment has a detailed operations list with HTTP status codes and response bodies; (3) Azure Monitor Diagnostic Logs—enable at resource level to stream database-level logs (errors, SQLInsights) to Log Analytics; (4) Azure SQL Connectivity Troubleshooter—portal tool that tests connectivity from the browser to the SQL endpoint and reports firewall/DNS/TLS issues. For IaC deployments (Terraform, Bicep), use `--debug` or `--verbose` flags to capture ARM response payloads. Common deployment errors: subnet not delegated (MI), quota exceeded (vCores), name already taken globally (logical server names are globally unique), or missing permissions (RBAC role missing on resource group).
fj: The Azure Activity Log contains detailed query-level execution information useful for troubleshooting slow queries during deployment.
fs: Activity Log records control-plane operations (resource create/update/delete), not query-level execution data. Query performance is in Query Store, DMVs, and diagnostic logs. Conflating these is a common source of confusion when troubleshooting.

q: What are Azure Elastic Jobs, how do they work, and how do you create and configure one?
a: Elastic Jobs are Azure SQL Database's native replacement for SQL Server Agent, designed for executing T-SQL scripts across a target group of databases (multiple databases, elastic pools, or shards). Architecture: an Elastic Job Agent is deployed as an Azure resource backed by a dedicated job database (a Standard S1 or higher Azure SQL DB). Job execution: the agent reads job definitions from the job database, distributes T-SQL steps to each target database using its own connection, and writes results back to the job database. Target groups can include specific databases, entire elastic pools, or databases in a resource group. Authentication to targets: the job step can authenticate via database-scoped credential (user + password) or, preferably, via user-assigned managed identity (UMI) for passwordless auth. To create: (1) `az sql elastic-jobs agent create` or Portal; (2) Create a target group and add targets; (3) Create a job with T-SQL steps pointing to the target group; (4) Create a schedule (cron-like: @hourly, @daily, or custom interval). Monitor job execution via `jobs.job_executions` and `jobs.job_step_executions` views in the job database.
fj: Elastic Jobs can be created using SQL Server Agent jobs that target Azure SQL Database through a Linked Server connection.
fs: SQL Server Agent is not available in Azure SQL Database and cannot connect to Azure SQL DB via Linked Server for job execution. Elastic Jobs is a completely separate Azure-native service that must be provisioned and configured independently.

code.label: Create elastic job target group, job, and step
lines: -- Create target group with a specific database
@command          = N'ALTER INDEX ALL ON dbo.Orders REBUILD;',

q: How do you create and configure database automation tasks in Azure SQL beyond Elastic Jobs?
a: Azure provides multiple automation paths for database tasks beyond Elastic Jobs: (1) Azure Automation runbooks—PowerShell or Python scripts hosted in an Automation Account, triggered on a schedule or webhook; support Az.Sql module for management operations (scale up DTUs, set firewall rules, backup management); good for cross-resource orchestration; (2) Azure Logic Apps—low-code workflow designer, has built-in SQL Server connector (can execute stored procedures or queries), integrates with approval flows and external APIs; (3) Azure Functions with timer trigger—serverless compute for lightweight scheduled T-SQL tasks; uses Microsoft.Data.SqlClient; scales to zero between runs; (4) Azure Data Factory / Synapse Pipelines—for ETL automation with scheduling, monitoring, and data movement; (5) GitHub Actions / Azure DevOps pipelines—for schema deployment automation using DACPAC or SqlPackage. Best practice: for pure T-SQL maintenance across multiple databases, Elastic Jobs is simplest. For complex orchestration (conditional logic, external API calls, ITSM integration), Logic Apps or Azure Automation are more expressive.
fj: Azure Automation runbooks can directly replace SQL Server Agent jobs in Azure SQL Managed Instance because they have full access to the SQL Agent APIs.
fs: SQL MI has native SQL Server Agent; Azure Automation runbooks do not replace it—they are an alternative orchestration layer for control-plane and management operations. SQL Agent on MI handles in-database maintenance jobs (T-SQL steps, SSIS) natively without needing external orchestration.

q: How do you configure alerts and notifications on automated database tasks in Azure SQL?
a: For Elastic Jobs: query `jobs.job_executions` in the job database and set up Azure Monitor Log Analytics or an Azure Function to detect failed executions and trigger an Action Group (email/SMS/webhook). Elastic Jobs do not have built-in email notifications—monitoring must be external. For Azure Automation runbooks: use the Automation Account's 'Alerts' feature in Azure Monitor, alerting on job failures by querying Azure Activity Log or creating a diagnostic setting to Log Analytics and alerting on `AzureActivity | where OperationName == "Start runbook job" and Status == "Failed"`. For Logic Apps: use the built-in run history and configure a parallel error-handling branch that sends an HTTP action to an email API or calls a webhook. For Azure SQL MI SQL Agent jobs: configure Database Mail + Operator + Job Notification as described previously. Cross-cutting approach: send all automation events to a central Azure Monitor Workspace, then create Scheduled Query Rule alerts that trigger Action Groups for any failure pattern, enabling Ops team ITSM ticket creation via webhook.
fj: Elastic Job failures automatically send email notifications if you set the job's notification setting to 'On Failure'.
fs: Elastic Jobs do not have an 'On Failure' email notification setting. All job monitoring must be implemented externally by querying the job execution metadata tables or integrating with Azure Monitor.

q: How do you troubleshoot automated database tasks (Elastic Jobs, Automation runbooks) when they fail silently or produce unexpected results?
a: For Elastic Jobs: (1) Query `jobs.job_executions` filtered by `lifecycle = 'Failed'` or `lifecycle = 'TimedOut'`; (2) Query `jobs.job_step_executions` joining on `job_execution_id` to get per-step error messages and target details; (3) Check `message` column for T-SQL error text; (4) Verify target credentials or managed identity has required database permissions on each target. For silent failures (job shows succeeded but data not updated): confirm the step's `command` is idempotent and no transactions rolled back silently; add explicit `RAISERROR` or `THROW` with non-zero exit code. For Azure Automation runbooks: check job output and error streams in the Azure Portal Jobs view; enable verbose logging (`$VerbosePreference = 'Continue'`) and stream logs to Log Analytics for querying. For Logic Apps: each run's action history shows input/output per step and error details for failed actions; use 'Re-run' on individual actions after fixing. Across all automation: add explicit logging (INSERT to a log table or send to Application Insights) inside each automation task so you have evidence of what ran and when.
fj: If an Elastic Job step fails, the job automatically retries the step indefinitely until it succeeds.
fs: Elastic Jobs have configurable retry policies (max retry attempts, retry interval) but default to a finite number of retries. Without explicit configuration, a failed step will not retry indefinitely—it will eventually mark the execution as failed and stop.

code.label: Query Elastic Job execution failures
lines: -- Run in the Elastic Job database

q: How do you choose an HA/DR strategy for Azure SQL based on RPO and RTO requirements?
a: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is the maximum tolerable data loss; RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the maximum acceptable downtime. Map these to Azure SQL capabilities: For RPO near-zero and RTO < 30 seconds—Active Geo-Replication (asynchronous log shipping to readable secondary, manual or auto failover) or Failover Groups (abstraction over geo-replication with automatic failover policy). For RPO minutes and RTO minutes—Point-in-Time Restore (PITR) is not suitable for DR but for accidental deletion recovery (RPO = 7-35 days of backup retention). For RPO hours and RTO hours—Long-term backup retention (LTR) to Azure Blob with weekly/monthly/yearly backups. For zone-redundant HA within a region—Zone-redundant configuration (Business Critical or Premium tier) replicates across availability zones with RPO = 0 and RTO < 5 seconds. For hybrid/SQL MI on-premises-to-Azure—Log shipping for cold standby (RPO hours) or Distributed Availability Groups for near-zero RPO. Always combine: zone-redundancy for in-region HA + failover group for cross-region DR + LTR for ransomware/accidental deletion protection.
fj: Point-in-Time Restore (PITR) is the recommended mechanism for meeting an RTO of 30 seconds in a DR scenario.
fs: PITR is designed for data recovery scenarios (accidental deletion, corruption), not DR failover. The PITR process takes minutes to hours depending on database size. For RTO of 30 seconds, Failover Groups with automatic failover policy or Business Critical zone-redundant configuration are the correct choices.

q: How do you evaluate HA/DR options for hybrid Azure SQL deployments?
a: Hybrid deployments mix on-premises SQL Server with Azure SQL resources. Key HA/DR options to evaluate: (1) Azure SQL Managed Instance Link (MI Link)—replicates a specific database from an on-premises SQL Server AG (SQL 2016+) to Azure SQL MI as a read-only replica; enables Azure-side reporting and provides a migration path with near-zero-downtime cutover; RPO near-zero while link is active. (2) Log Shipping to Azure SQL MI—traditional log backup copy/restore to MI, RPO in minutes to hours; simpler setup but less automation. (3) Distributed Availability Groups—extend an on-premises AG to include an Azure VM AG member; complex but enables high-fidelity hybrid HA. (4) Azure Arc-enabled SQL MI—run a cloud-managed MI on-premises with local HA via Kubernetes-native AGs. Evaluate: RTO/RPO requirements, licensing costs (Azure Hybrid Benefit for SQL VMs), network bandwidth for log volume, and operational complexity. Azure SQL MI Link is the current recommended path for hybrid DR as it is the simplest near-zero-RPO option.
fj: Azure SQL Managed Instance Link is a bi-directional active-active replication feature allowing writes on both ends simultaneously.
fs: MI Link is unidirectional—the on-premises SQL Server AG is the primary (writable) and the Azure SQL MI is a read-only replica. Bi-directional writes require full failover through the link, after which the MI becomes the new primary.

q: What Azure-specific HA/DR solutions does Azure SQL Database provide natively, and how do they differ from on-premises SQL Server HA?
a: Azure SQL Database provides built-in HA for all service tiers: General Purpose uses remote storage (Azure Premium Storage with zone-redundant replication) for data files and a hot standby for the compute layer—failover is transparent, typically < 30 seconds. Business Critical uses a built-in Always On Availability Group with 3-4 replicas in the same region, providing RPO = 0 and RTO < 5 seconds with one readable secondary available for read scale. Compared to on-premises: in Azure, you do not manage quorum witnesses, Windows Server Failover Cluster configuration, shared storage, or listener DNS—all are abstracted. Additional Azure-specific solutions: (1) Zone-redundant databases (Business Critical/Premium/Hyperscale) spread replicas across Availability Zones for regional datacenter failure tolerance; (2) Active Geo-Replication extends replication cross-region with a readable secondary; (3) Auto-Failover Groups add DNS-based listener and automatic failover policy. On-premises SQL Server requires AlwaysOn AG, FCI, or log shipping configured manually; Azure SQL provides equivalent capabilities as managed platform services.
fj: All Azure SQL Database service tiers provide the same built-in HA SLA with zero RPO using in-memory Always On AG replicas.
fs: General Purpose tier uses a hot-standby compute + remote storage model (not an in-memory AG) with potential brief data loss during storage failover (RPO is typically seconds). Only Business Critical and Hyperscale use multi-replica AG technology with RPO near zero within a region.

q: How do you design a backup and restore strategy for Azure SQL Database, including retention settings and backup types?
a: Azure SQL Database performs automated backups: full backups weekly, differential backups every 12-24 hours, and transaction log backups every 5-10 minutes. These are stored in Azure RA-GRS (geo-redundant) blob storage by default, or LRS/ZRS if configured. Short-term retention (PITR retention) is configurable from 1 to 35 days (default 7 days for most tiers). Long-term retention (LTR) allows retaining full backups for up to 10 years using a weekly/monthly/yearly retention policy—stored in separate RA-GRS storage. Design considerations: set PITR retention to at least the longest transaction or reporting cycle (e.g., 30 days for monthly close); set LTR for compliance requirements (financial records: 7 years, health data: potentially longer). For SQL MI and SQL Server on Azure VMs, native T-SQL backups to Azure Blob Storage (`BACKUP TO URL`) supplement automated backups. Use Azure Backup for SQL on Azure VMs to get centralised policy management. Test restores regularly to validate backup integrity—in Azure SQL DB, run a test restore to a new database name via Portal or CLI.
fj: In Azure SQL Database, you can create an on-demand manual full backup using T-SQL BACKUP DATABASE command at any time.
fs: Azure SQL Database does not support the T-SQL BACKUP DATABASE command—backups are fully automated and managed by the platform. You can copy a database using Database Copy or export a BACPAC, but these are not backup operations. Manual T-SQL backups are supported in SQL Managed Instance and SQL Server on Azure VMs.

q: How do you perform a backup and restore using native T-SQL tools for Azure SQL Managed Instance?
a: SQL Managed Instance supports native T-SQL `BACKUP` and `RESTORE` commands targeting Azure Blob Storage URLs. You must first create a database-scoped credential using a Shared Access Signature (SAS) token or a managed identity. For backup: `BACKUP DATABASE [mydb] TO URL = 'https://storageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/backups/mydb.bak' WITH CREDENTIAL = 'myCredential', COMPRESSION, CHECKSUM`. For restore: `RESTORE DATABASE [mydb] FROM URL = '...' WITH CREDENTIAL = 'myCredential', MOVE 'mydb_data' TO '/var/opt/mssql/data/mydb.mdf', MOVE 'mydb_log' TO '/var/opt/mssql/data/mydb_log.ldf'`. Use `WITH NORECOVERY` for multi-file restores of differential and log backups, then `WITH RECOVERY` on the final step. The copy-only backup (`WITH COPY_ONLY`) creates an independent backup that does not affect the differential baseline. SQL MI also supports automated managed backups (PITR) in parallel—native backups are additive for long-term retention or cross-instance migration.
fj: Restoring a database to Azure SQL Managed Instance using RESTORE FROM URL always requires the database to be offline first.
fs: For a new database restore (the database does not yet exist on the target MI), it does not need to be offline—you are creating it fresh. For restoring over an existing database, you can use `WITH REPLACE` to overwrite. In most migration scenarios you are restoring to a new database name, so no offline requirement applies.

code.label: Backup and restore to Azure Blob Storage from SQL MI
lines: -- Create credential for Blob Storage SAS

q: How do you perform a point-in-time restore (PITR) for an Azure SQL Database, and what are its limitations?
a: PITR restores an Azure SQL Database to any point within the retention period (1-35 days, default 7 days) by replaying automated backups. To perform: Portal—select the database > Restore > choose restore point on a timeline slider > provide new database name (you cannot restore over the existing live database) > click Review + Create. CLI: `az sql db restore --dest-name NewDB --resource-group myRG --server myServer --name SalesDB --time '2026-06-09T02:00:00'`. The restored database lands as a new database alongside the original. After validation, optionally rename or swap the databases. Key limitations: (1) Cannot restore to the same name/replace in place—always creates a new database; (2) Retention cap at 35 days (use LTR for longer); (3) Cannot restore to a time when the database was a Failover Group secondary (restore from the primary's history); (4) The restore creates a database with the firewall, backup retention, and tier settings of the original at the point of restore—verify post-restore configuration; (5) Restores within the same logical server and region only (cross-region restore requires geo-restore from geo-redundant backup).
fj: Point-in-time restore in Azure SQL Database restores the database in place, overwriting the current version with the selected snapshot.
fs: PITR always creates a new database—it cannot restore in place over the running database. This is by design to allow side-by-side validation before switching over, and to avoid data loss if the wrong restore point is chosen.

code.label: Perform PITR via Azure CLI

q: How do you configure long-term backup retention (LTR) for Azure SQL Database?
a: Long-term retention (LTR) extends backup storage beyond the 35-day PITR window by keeping full weekly backups for up to 10 years. LTR backups are automatically stored in Azure RA-GRS Blob Storage and are billed separately from PITR storage. Configuration: Portal—SQL Database > Manage Backups > Long-term retention > Configure retention policy. Four policy fields: W (weekly, how many weeks to keep the weekly backup), M (monthly, how many months to keep the first backup of each month), Y (yearly, how many years to keep the first backup of each year), and WeekOfYear (which week of the year to designate as the 'yearly' backup). CLI: `az sql db ltr-policy set --server myServer --database myDB --resource-group myRG --weekly-retention P4W --monthly-retention P12M --yearly-retention P5Y --week-of-year 26`. To restore from LTR: `az sql db ltr-backup restore` pointing to a specific LTR backup name. LTR is available for Azure SQL Database and SQL MI. Use cases: compliance regulations requiring backup retention longer than one month (financial, healthcare, legal).
fj: Long-term retention backups in Azure SQL Database are differential backups taken every day to minimise storage size.
fs: LTR stores full backups only (weekly or as configured by WeekOfYear), not differentials. The advantage is full self-containment of each backup—you can restore any LTR backup independently without a base + differentials chain.

code.label: Set LTR policy via Azure CLI
lines: # Keep 4 weekly, 12 monthly, 5 yearly (week 26 = yearly snapshot)

q: How does active geo-replication work in Azure SQL Database, and how do you configure and initiate a failover?
a: Active geo-replication continuously replicates an Azure SQL Database to up to four readable secondary databases in different Azure regions using asynchronous transaction log shipping built on Always On AG technology. The secondary is always readable (read scale) and is a full database copy, not just a backup. To configure: Portal—database > Geo-Replication > Add secondary region > choose target server > select Service Tier. CLI: `az sql db replica create --resource-group myRG --server primaryServer --name SalesDB --partner-server secondaryServer --partner-resource-group secRG`. Failover types: planned failover (no data loss—`az sql db replica set-primary` after replication is caught up) and unplanned/forced failover (potential data loss—same command but initiated when primary is unreachable). After failover, the original primary becomes a secondary if it recovers. Geo-replication is database-level; for server-level DNS abstraction, use Failover Groups on top. Active geo-replication is not available for SQL MI (MI uses Failover Groups natively with distributed AGs).
fj: Active geo-replication for Azure SQL Database provides synchronous replication, guaranteeing zero data loss for any failover.
