fs: Active geo-replication uses asynchronous replication—the primary commits transactions without waiting for the secondary to confirm, so there is potential for data loss in an unplanned failover (lag depends on network latency and write volume). Synchronous replication (RPO = 0) requires Business Critical zone-redundant configuration within a region.

code.label: Create geo-replica and perform planned failover
lines: # Initiate planned failover (makes secondary the new primary)

q: What are Auto-Failover Groups in Azure SQL and how do you configure one for automatic failover?
a: Auto-Failover Groups are an abstraction layer over active geo-replication that provides: a single read-write listener endpoint (<group-name>.database.windows.net) that always points to the primary, and a read-only listener (<group-name>.secondary.database.windows.net) that points to the secondary. Applications connect to the listener DNS name rather than a specific server, so failover is transparent. Configuration: Portal—logical server > Failover Groups > Add Group > specify secondary server > choose Manual or Automatic failover policy > set grace period (0-3600 seconds before automatic failover triggers after primary loss). CLI: `az sql failover-group create --name fgSalesDB --server primaryServer --partner-server secondaryServer --failover-policy Automatic --grace-period 60`. All databases added to the group are replicated together; you can add multiple databases. For Azure SQL MI, Failover Groups use Distributed Availability Groups internally. After failover, the group's DNS listener automatically resolves to the new primary—no application connection string change needed. Use paired regions for best network performance.
fj: Each database in an Auto-Failover Group must be individually configured with its own geo-replication relationship before adding to the group.
fs: Adding a database to a Failover Group automatically creates and manages the geo-replication relationship—you do not need to pre-configure active geo-replication separately. The group handles replica creation and synchronisation as part of group membership.

code.label: Create auto-failover group with automatic policy
lines: # Add a database to the failover group

q: How do you configure an Always On Failover Cluster Instance (FCI) on Azure Virtual Machines?
a: An FCI on Azure VMs provides instance-level HA (entire SQL Server instance fails over, not just databases) using Windows Server Failover Cluster (WSFC) and shared storage. Architecture requires: 2+ Azure VMs in an Availability Set or Availability Zones, an Azure Shared Disk or Azure NetApp Files or Storage Spaces Direct (S2D) for shared storage, and the WSFC feature installed on each node. Steps: (1) Deploy VMs in an Availability Set with premium storage; (2) Join all nodes to the same Active Directory domain; (3) Enable WSFC feature and create cluster with Azure-specific witness (Azure Blob Storage witness recommended over file share); (4) Attach Azure Shared Disk to all nodes (Premium SSD v2 or Ultra Disk supports multi-node attachment); (5) Install SQL Server FCI from the setup wizard choosing 'New SQL Server Failover Cluster Installation' on node 1 and 'Add Node to SQL Server Failover Cluster' on subsequent nodes; (6) Configure the FCI IP as an Azure Internal Load Balancer VIP (Floating IP enabled). Azure-specific caveat: FCI does not support multi-region; for cross-region DR, combine FCI with geo-replication or log shipping.
fj: An FCI on Azure VMs uses active geo-replication under the hood to provide shared storage access across nodes.
fs: FCI uses actual shared storage (Azure Shared Disk or S2D) accessed by WSFC cluster nodes, not geo-replication. Geo-replication is a database-level asynchronous replication mechanism for Azure SQL Database PaaS, completely separate from the shared-storage FCI architecture used on SQL Server IaaS VMs.

q: How do you configure log shipping in SQL Server / Azure SQL Managed Instance, and what are its RPO/RTO characteristics?
a: Log shipping involves three SQL Agent jobs: a backup job on the primary (copies transaction log backups to a share), a copy job on the secondary (reads log backups from the share), and a restore job on the secondary (applies logs to the secondary database in STANDBY or NORECOVERY mode). To configure in SSMS: right-click the database > Properties > Transaction Log Shipping > check 'Enable this as a primary database in a log shipping configuration' > configure backup settings, secondary servers, and schedule intervals. RPO is determined by the backup frequency (typically 15-60 minutes, configurable). RTO is the time to restore outstanding logs plus time to bring the secondary online (minutes). The secondary can be left in STANDBY mode (readable) or NORECOVERY (not accessible). Log shipping is simpler than AGs and supported across SQL Server versions and editions (including Standard Edition, unlike AGs). For Azure SQL MI, log shipping uses native backup/restore to Azure Blob Storage URLs with SQL Agent jobs. Limitations: no automatic failover, manual intervention required for cutover, and RPO is bounded by the backup schedule gap.
fj: Log shipping provides automatic failover to the secondary when the primary becomes unavailable.
fs: Log shipping has no automatic failover mechanism. The secondary must be manually brought online by running `RESTORE DATABASE ... WITH RECOVERY` after confirming the primary is down. This is a key reason to prefer Failover Groups or Always On AGs when automatic failover is required.

q: How do you monitor and troubleshoot an HA/DR solution for Azure SQL Database?
a: Monitoring HA/DR covers both the replication health and failover readiness. For Failover Groups and geo-replication: Azure Monitor metric 'Replication lag' on the secondary database (target: < 5 seconds for critical workloads); Portal Geo-Replication view shows replication state (Seeding, Catching-up, Synchronized); CLI: `az sql db replica list-links` shows replication state and lag. Alerts: create Azure Monitor metric alert on replication_lag > threshold, routing to Action Group. For SQL MI Failover Groups: query `sys.dm_geo_replication_link_status` DMV for lag and health state. Troubleshooting common issues: (1) Replication lag growing—check primary write workload; scale primary DTUs/vCores or reduce log generation; (2) Secondary in 'Seeding' state indefinitely—check network connectivity and firewall between regions; verify secondary server exists and has capacity; (3) Automatic failover triggered unexpectedly—review 'grace period' setting; check Azure service health for the primary region; (4) DNS not resolving to new primary after failover—TTL on failover group DNS is 30 seconds; wait or flush DNS client cache; (5) Application not reconnecting after failover—ensure retry logic with exponential backoff in connection code.
fj: If the replication lag for a Failover Group increases, the solution is to decrease the PITR retention period to reduce backup I/O on the primary.
fs: Replication lag is driven by the volume of transaction log data generated on the primary and network bandwidth to the secondary—not by PITR backup activity. Reducing PITR retention affects backup storage costs but does not reduce log generation or replication lag.

code.label: Check geo-replication link status and lag on SQL MI
